questions.questionText: The functional unit of the kidney is the:
questions.explanation: Each kidney contains about 1 million nephrons.

questions.questionText: Most of the filtered sodium and water is reabsorbed in the:
questions.explanation: About 65% of filtered Na+ and H2O is reabsorbed in the PCT.

questions.questionText: Glucose is normally completely reabsorbed in the proximal tubule via:
questions.explanation: SGLT2 (and SGLT1) cotransport glucose with Na+ on the apical membrane.
JG cells in the afferent arteriole secrete renin in response to low BP, sympathetic tone, or low NaCl.

questions.questionText: Antidiuretic hormone (ADH) primarily acts on which part of the nephron to increase water reabsorption?
questions.explanation: ADH inserts aquaporin-2 channels into the principal cells of the collecting duct.

questions.optionD: Increase sodium reabsorption and potassium secretion
questions.explanation: Aldosterone acts on principal cells in the late distal tubule and collecting duct.

questions.questionText: The macula densa senses changes in:
questions.optionC: NaCl load in the distal tubule
questions.optionD: Blood pressure in the afferent arteriole
questions.explanation: Macula densa cells monitor NaCl delivery and mediate tubuloglomerular feedback.

questions.questionText: Which segment of the loop of Henle is impermeable to water but actively reabsorbs NaCl?
questions.explanation: The thick ascending limb is the 'diluting segment' and has the Na-K-2Cl cotransporter.

questions.questionText: Inulin clearance is used to clinical estimate:
questions.explanation: Inulin is freely filtered, and neither reabsorbed nor secreted.

questions.questionText: Para-aminohippuric acid (PAH) clearance is used to estimate:
questions.explanation: PAH is completely cleared from the plasma by filtration and active secretion.

questions.questionText: In Renal Physiology, which statement is most accurate regarding the core definition and diagnostic concept?
questions.optionA: It reflects the most exam-relevant definition used in standard Physiology texts
questions.optionB: It is an obsolete definition no longer used in clinical practice
questions.optionC: It is used only in pediatric population and never in adults
questions.optionD: It has no diagnostic significance in NEET-PG pattern questions
questions.explanation: This tests foundational definition-level knowledge from standard Physiology references for Renal Physiology. Subject: Physiology. Chapter: Renal Physiology.

questions.questionText: The most important etiology/risk factor to suspect first in a typical Renal Physiology scenario is:
questions.optionA: The common high-yield risk factor repeatedly asked in entrance exams
questions.optionB: A rare cause seen only in isolated case reports
questions.optionD: An etiology excluded by routine first-line workup
questions.explanation: NEET-PG and INI-CET focus on common, high-yield causes before rare differentials. Subject: Physiology. Chapter: Renal Physiology.
